metadata.summary: Production AWS service selection (serverless vs containers), hybrid architecture, event-driven patterns, and pre-deployment checklists.

content.title: AWS Architecture Services
content.summary: Production-ready patterns for AWS service selection, integration, and deployment. Covers serverless vs containers, EventBridge event-driven design, the Well-Architected six pillars, and Terraform configurations for EKS, RDS Aurora, ALB, S3+CloudFront, and API Gateway. The default rule: Lambda for variable/spiky traffic under 15 minutes; containers for steady high-volume 24/7 workloads; hybrid for everything in between.

why.p: Choosing the wrong compute service creates wasted cost (over-provisioned containers idling at 5% CPU) or reliability failures (Lambda timing out on a 20-minute report). Explicit decision criteria, production Terraform with hardcoded security defaults (no public S3, KMS encryption, multi-AZ, IAM authentication) and a pre-deployment checklist eliminate guesswork and make every change reviewable.

list.item: Selecting compute service (Lambda vs ECS Fargate vs EKS) for a new workload
Designing event-driven architectures with EventBridge, SNS, SQS, or Step Functions
Applying AWS Well-Architected Framework review to existing infrastructure
Writing Terraform for EKS clusters, Aurora, ALB, CloudFront, or API Gateway
Performing cost optimization (Graviton migration, Spot, Reserved, Instance Scheduler)
Reviewing a pull request that adds or modifies AWS infrastructure
Onboarding a new account and applying baseline tags, CloudTrail, and AWS Config
GCP or Azure architecture decisions — use gcp-arch-patterns or the Azure methodology instead
Terraform syntax basics — use terraform-basics for HCL fundamentals
VPC and networking design — use aws-networking for subnet, SG, and TGW patterns
S3 bucket policies and lifecycle rules — use aws-s3-storage for storage-specific patterns
Lambda function packaging and cold start tuning — use aws-lambda
EC2 or ECS task definitions and capacity providers — use aws-ec2-ecs

how-to.step: Read the workload requirements: traffic shape (spiky vs steady), max execution time, GPU need, persistent connections, regulatory constraints. Write them down before opening the AWS console.
Apply the compute decision tree: if max-execution > 15 min OR GPU OR websockets OR steady high-volume 24/7 then containers; otherwise default to Lambda.
Apply the orchestration choice: team has Kubernetes expertise and needs Helm or multi-cloud → EKS; otherwise → ECS Fargate.

Apply the integration pattern: cross-service routing → EventBridge custom bus; multi-step workflow with retries/compensation → Step Functions; async buffering single consumer → SQS; fan-out broadcast → SNS.
Apply the database choice: relational OLTP → Aurora (PostgreSQL preferred); key-value or document with predictable single-digit ms latency → DynamoDB; analytical → Redshift or Athena.
Generate Terraform from the templates in this folder and pin all defaults: KMS encryption, multi-AZ, private subnets, IAM authentication, deletion protection on prod, Graviton instances.
Run the pre-deployment checklist (below) and the verify section before plan/apply.
Run terraform fmt, terraform validate, tflint, and checkov; fix every finding before submitting the PR.

rule.statement: Choose Lambda when traffic is variable or spiky, execution is under 15 minutes, cold start under 2 seconds is acceptable, and GPU is not required.
rule.rationale: Lambda costs per invocation; idle time costs nothing. Containers cost per provisioned hour regardless of traffic.

rule.statement: Choose containers (EKS or ECS Fargate) for steady 24/7 workloads above 1 million requests per day, long-running processes, GPU requirements, persistent websocket connections, or when the Kubernetes ecosystem is needed.
rule.rationale: Reserved container capacity is cheaper than Lambda at high sustained throughput. GPU workloads require EC2-backed compute. Lambda has a 15-minute hard timeout and no persistent connections.

rule.statement: Default to a hybrid architecture: Lambda for event-driven and sporadic workloads, containers for always-on services. Document the boundary in an architecture decision record.
rule.rationale: Most teams run hybrid architectures. The right question is which workload belongs where, not a binary choice.

example.title: E-commerce hybrid architecture
example.note: EKS for the main web API (steady traffic, websockets for live cart sync); Lambda for order notification webhooks (spiky traffic, <30 second invocations); Step Functions for the multi-stage payment workflow (stateful with compensation on refund); SQS in front of order-processing Lambda for back-pressure.

antipattern.description: Running all workloads as Lambda including a websocket server, ML inference at >5GB model size, or a 20-minute nightly report.
antipattern.reason: Lambda has a 15-minute timeout, no persistent connections, no GPU support, and a 10GB ephemeral storage cap.

rule.statement: Choose Fargate (ECS or EKS) when the team lacks Kubernetes expertise or wants to avoid node management overhead.
rule.rationale: Fargate is fully managed; no patching, scaling, or capacity planning for nodes.

rule.statement: Choose EKS when multi-cloud portability, Helm ecosystem, GitOps with ArgoCD, or advanced scheduling is required.
rule.rationale: EKS uses standard Kubernetes APIs, enabling migration to other clouds or on-prem clusters.

rule.statement: Use RDS Aurora PostgreSQL for relational OLTP; use DynamoDB for key-value or document access patterns requiring single-digit millisecond latency at any scale; use Redshift for analytical queries over >1TB.
rule.rationale: Aurora provides storage auto-scaling and read replicas. DynamoDB avoids connection pooling limits at high concurrency. Redshift columnar storage is purpose-built for OLAP.

rule.statement: Use EventBridge for decoupled cross-service routing; use Step Functions for stateful orchestration; use SQS for async buffering with retry; use SNS for fan-out notifications.
rule.rationale: Each service solves a distinct coordination problem. Using SQS for routing or SNS for stateful workflows creates operational complexity.

rule.statement: Apply all six Well-Architected pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability.
rule.rationale: Skipping any pillar creates technical debt: missing Security leads to breaches; missing Cost Optimization leads to uncontrolled spend.

rule.statement: Default to Graviton (ARM64) instances for all new compute resources; require an explicit comment to justify x86 usage.
rule.rationale: Graviton provides roughly 20% lower cost and comparable or better performance for most workloads.

rule.statement: Use a custom event bus (not default) for application events; enable event archive with 30-day retention for replay capability.
rule.rationale: The default event bus mixes AWS service events with application events. Archiving enables replay during outages.

rule.statement: Always configure a dead-letter queue and retry policy on every EventBridge target rule.
rule.rationale: Without DLQ, failed event deliveries are silently dropped. EventBridge retries up to 185 times over 24 hours by default.

example.title: EventBridge target with DLQ and retry policy

rule.statement: Use SQS Standard for async decoupling with at-least-once delivery; use SQS FIFO when message ordering matters; use SNS for fan-out to multiple subscribers.
rule.rationale: SQS provides buffering and retry; SNS delivers immediately to all subscribers without buffering.

rule.statement: Use Step Functions for workflows with more than 2 sequential steps, error handling, or human approval gates.
rule.rationale: Chaining Lambda functions directly via code creates invisible retry logic and poor observability. Step Functions provides visual state machines with built-in retry/catch.

antipattern.description: Using SNS as a queue replacement for a single consumer.
antipattern.reason: SNS has no retention — if the consumer is down, messages are lost. Use SQS for single-consumer reliable delivery.

rule.statement: Use EventBridge choreography for loosely coupled, independently deployable services; use Step Functions orchestration for multi-step transactions requiring compensation (saga pattern).
rule.rationale: Choreography reduces coupling but makes flow hard to trace. Orchestration centralizes visibility at the cost of coupling to the orchestrator.

rule.statement: Deploy EKS cluster in private subnets with IRSA enabled, secrets encryption via KMS, and managed node groups using Graviton instances.
rule.rationale: Private subnets prevent direct internet exposure. IRSA eliminates long-lived access keys on nodes. KMS encryption protects secrets in etcd.

rule.statement: Enable at least CoreDNS, kube-proxy, VPC CNI, and EBS CSI driver as managed add-ons.
rule.rationale: Managed add-ons receive security patches automatically without manual intervention.

rule.statement: Always deploy Aurora with Multi-AZ (minimum 2 instances), encryption at rest via KMS, IAM authentication enabled, and deletion protection on prod.
rule.rationale: Single-instance Aurora has no automatic failover. Deletion protection prevents accidental data loss in production.

rule.statement: Set backup retention to 30 days in production; enable Performance Insights and Enhanced Monitoring with 60-second granularity.
rule.rationale: 30-day retention meets most compliance requirements. Performance Insights identifies slow queries proactively.

rule.statement: Redirect HTTP port 80 to HTTPS 443 with SSL policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06; enable access logs to S3 with 90-day lifecycle.
rule.rationale: TLS 1.3 policy disables weak cipher suites. Access logs are required for security audits and debugging.

rule.statement: Use CloudFront Origin Access Control (OAC) for S3 origins, not legacy OAI; enable HTTP/2 and HTTP/3.
rule.rationale: OAC uses SigV4 signing and supports SSE-KMS, which OAI does not.

rule.statement: Tag all resources with Project, Environment, Owner, and ManagedBy=terraform; enable CloudTrail (multi-region trail) and AWS Config in every account.
rule.rationale: Tags enable cost allocation and compliance reporting. CloudTrail provides the audit trail required for SOC2/PCI.

rule.statement: Use Spot instances for fault-tolerant batch workloads, Reserved capacity for steady-state, and Instance Scheduler to stop non-prod environments overnight.
rule.rationale: Instance Scheduler can save up to 75% on non-prod costs. Spot pricing is 60-90% cheaper than On-Demand for interruptible workloads.

antipattern.description: Overly permissive IAM with wildcard (*) resource ARNs in production.
antipattern.reason: Wildcards grant unintended access to all resources, violating least privilege and failing security audits.
An AZ outage takes the entire workload offline. Multi-AZ is required for any SLA above 99.9%.

checklist.check: Workload requirements documented: traffic shape, max execution time, GPU/connection needs, regulatory constraints.
Compute service selected against the decision tree; choice recorded in an ADR or PR description.
Integration pattern selected (EventBridge / Step Functions / SQS / SNS) with a one-line justification.
All resources tagged with Project, Environment, Owner, ManagedBy=terraform.
All compute defaults to Graviton (ARM64) or contains an explicit x86 justification comment.
S3 buckets: Block Public Access enabled, default encryption SSE-KMS, versioning on for prod data buckets.
RDS / Aurora: Multi-AZ, KMS encryption, IAM auth enabled, deletion protection on prod, backup retention 30 days.
EKS: private subnets, IRSA, KMS secrets encryption, managed node groups, managed add-ons (CoreDNS, kube-proxy, VPC CNI, EBS CSI).
ALB: HTTP→HTTPS redirect, TLS 1.3 policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06, access logs to S3.
CloudFront: Origin Access Control (OAC), HTTP/2 + HTTP/3 enabled, custom error pages.
EventBridge: custom bus (not default), 30-day archive, DLQ + retry_policy on every target.
Lambda: timeout set to realistic value, reserved concurrency declared, DLQ on async invocations.
IAM: no wildcard (*) resource ARNs in production policies; least-privilege enforced.
CloudTrail multi-region trail, AWS Config recorder, GuardDuty enabled in every account.
Cost guardrails: Budgets alerting at 80% and 100%, Instance Scheduler for non-prod.
terraform fmt, terraform validate, tflint, and checkov all PASS with zero high/critical findings.

commands.command: aws s3api list-buckets --query "Buckets[].Name" --output text | xargs -n1 -I{} aws s3api get-bucket-policy-status --bucket {} 2>/dev/null
aws ce get-cost-and-usage --time-period Start=$(date -u -d '30 days ago' +%Y-%m-%d),End=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%d) --granularity MONTHLY --metrics UnblendedCost --group-by Type=DIMENSION,Key=SERVICE

prompt.purpose: Generate the AWS architecture decision record for a new workload

content.prompt:
You are an AWS solutions architect. Produce a one-page Architecture Decision Record using ONLY the template below. Do not invent fields. Do not skip sections. If a field cannot be answered from the inputs, write "TBD — needs <specific input>".
INPUTS: - workload_name: <name> - traffic_shape: <spiky | steady | hybrid> - peak_rps: <number> - max_execution_seconds: <number> - needs_gpu: <true | false> - needs_websockets: <true | false> - data_class: <public | internal | confidential | regulated> - region: <aws-region> - environment: <dev | staging | prod>
TEMPLATE: # ADR — <workload_name>
## Decision - Compute: <Lambda | ECS Fargate | EKS> because <reason citing decision tree> - Database: <Aurora PostgreSQL | DynamoDB | Redshift> because <reason> - Integration: <EventBridge | Step Functions | SQS | SNS> because <reason> - Defaults applied: Graviton, Multi-AZ, KMS, IAM auth, private subnets, OAC.
## Well-Architected check - Operational Excellence: <one line> - Security: <one line> - Reliability: <one line> - Performance Efficiency: <one line> - Cost Optimization: <one line> - Sustainability: <one line>
## Cost estimate (monthly, USD) - Compute: $<n> - Storage: $<n> - Data transfer: $<n> - Total: $<n>
## Risks - <one line> - <one line>
## Alternatives considered - <option>: rejected because <reason>

verify.step: Run terraform fmt -recursive and confirm no diffs remain (git diff --exit-code).
Run terraform validate in every module folder and confirm zero errors.
Run tflint --recursive and confirm zero findings.
Run checkov -d . --framework terraform and confirm zero high or critical findings; document any suppressions in checkov.yaml with a justification comment.
Run terraform plan and confirm the resource count matches the change description; no unexpected destroy operations on stateful resources (RDS, S3, DynamoDB).
Verify each item in the checklist above is satisfied; check off in the PR description.
Confirm all S3 buckets have Block Public Access enabled: aws s3api get-public-access-block --bucket <name> should return all four flags true.
Confirm RDS encryption: aws rds describe-db-instances --query "DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,StorageEncrypted]" returns true for every prod instance.
Confirm EventBridge targets have DLQ: aws events list-targets-by-rule --rule <name> --query "Targets[].DeadLetterConfig.Arn" returns a non-null ARN for each.
Confirm CloudTrail multi-region trail is logging: aws cloudtrail describe-trails --query "trailList[?IsMultiRegionTrail==\`true\`].Name" returns at least one trail.
Run a synthetic load test or canary against the new endpoint and verify CloudWatch alarms fire as expected; resolve any noisy alarms before merge.

template.purpose: Common Terraform variables (project, environment, region, VPC CIDR, AZs)
Common tags and name prefix locals
VPC module with NAT gateway, flow logs, Kubernetes subnet tags
KMS keys for EKS, RDS, S3 encryption
ALB, app, Lambda, RDS security group templates
ECS task execution, ECS task, Step Functions IAM role templates
CloudWatch alarms for ALB, RDS, Lambda with SNS topic
